preFix.title: Patching a spalled face brick
preFix.subtitle: Cut out and replace a frost-damaged brick in an exterior wall
preFix.excerpt: How to cut out a spalled or frost-damaged face brick from an exterior wall, shape a matching replacement, bed it in fresh mortar, and point the new joint to match the existing work. Covers identifying which bricks need replacing, cutting out without disturbing adjacent brickwork, and colour-matching the mortar.
preFix.sourceNotes: BS EN 771-1: Specification for masonry units (clay). BS 3921: Specification for clay bricks. Brick Development Association technical notes (open-access). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. City and Guilds Level 2 Bricklaying.

content.text: brick has lost the hard, frost-resistant face that protects the softer core. Left in place, the exposed core absorbs water rapidly and the spalling spreads to adjacent bricks within a few winters. Replacing isolated spalled bricks is straightforward; the work must be done dry and above freezing to allow the mortar to cure. Matching the brick colour and the mortar colour to the existing wall takes more care than the physical replacement.

A matching replacement brick, take a sample of the existing brick to a reclamation yard or builders' merchant. Colour, texture, size (British standard is 215 × 102.5 × 65 mm), and profile should all match. A sand-and-cement bedding mortar to match the existing joint colour: usually 4 parts soft sand to 1 part Portland cement, but the colour varies widely with the sand source; buy from the same batch as any existing pointing work, or test a small patch and compare dry.
Step 1, cut out the damaged brick

Fit a masonry disc to the angle grinder and score around all four mortar joints of the spalled brick to a depth of 15 mm. Score, do not cut through: cutting deep into the adjoining mortar risks cracking the adjacent bricks. Wear eye protection and hearing protection; the angle grinder will throw chips. Switch to the SDS drill with a flat chisel bit and break the spalled brick out in pieces by chiselling from the centre outward. Work toward the scored lines rather than away from them; hammering directly at the joints risks cracking adjacent bricks.

Clear all old mortar and debris from the opening with the chisel and brush. The back wall of the chase (the masonry behind where the replacement brick sits) must be clean and sound. Blow or brush out all loose material. Wet the inside of the chase with water before bedding the new brick; a dry chase will pull the water from the mortar before it sets.

Mix the bedding mortar to a stiff paste; it should hold its shape when squeezed. Butter the back of the chase with mortar to a depth that positions the face of the replacement brick flush with (not proud of) the surrounding brickwork face. Butter all four sides of the replacement brick with mortar. Slide the brick into the chase and press it home; check it is flush on all sides using a straight batten held across the adjacent brickwork. Tap lightly with the handle of the filling knife to adjust; do not use a hammer directly on the face.
Allow the bedding mortar to stiffen slightly (45 to 60 minutes), then press fresh mortar into the face joints with the 50 mm filling knife. Form the

profile to match the existing wall; for a weathered joint, draw the back edge of the filling knife along the joint at an angle to slope the mortar so rain runs off. For a flush joint, drag the knife flat. For a recessed joint, use the handle of the filling knife or a piece of wooden dowel to push the joint back 3 to 4 mm from the face. Leave the pointing slightly proud of the final profile; it will be dressed back when it has hardened enough to take the striking tool.

Keep the new work damp for 3 days in hot or dry conditions by covering with damp hessian or misting lightly twice a day; rapid drying weakens mortar. Once the mortar has set fully (7 days minimum), brush any efflorescence from the new brick face with a stiff dry brush. Do not acid-wash a new mortar joint until at least 28 days after completion.

If no matching brick is available, take one from a hidden area of the same wall, such as inside a garage or under a window cill. Use it in the visible position and put the nearest-match modern brick in the hidden spot. Colour differences are less noticeable out of sight.

attrs.fix: Remove the brick before the mortar sets. Clear more material from the back of the chase. If the replacement brick is genuinely thicker, sand the back face on the sanding block to reduce it by the required amount.
attrs.cause: Insufficient mortar was removed from the back of the chase, or the replacement brick is thicker than the original.
attrs.symptom: Replacement brick sits proud of the surrounding wall face

attrs.fix: Allow the new mortar to fully dry (several weeks) before assessing; fresh mortar always looks darker than cured mortar. If the colour still does not match, rake out the pointing to 15 mm depth and re-point using mortar with a higher sand content or a coloured mortar additive to match the existing.
attrs.cause: Different sand source or cement-to-sand ratio producing a different grey or buff tone.
attrs.symptom: New mortar joint is a noticeably different colour from the existing wall

attrs.fix: Assess whether the cracks are cosmetic (surface hairlines only) or structural. Hairline cracks in bricks can be pointed over. A brick with a crack through its centre should also be removed and replaced using the same method.
attrs.cause: Angle grinder cut too deep into the mortar joint; chisel forces transferred to the adjacent masonry.
attrs.symptom: Adjacent bricks cracked during removal of the damaged brick
